etymology: PIE *h₂en- (to breathe/live)
Latin manere (to remain/stay) has -ane
Latin animus (directed will); Japanese に (ni = toward, directional particle)
Sanskrit apad (to move on foot)
★★ Sanskrit āsa (to sit, to be present); PIE *h₁es- (to be, to exist); Japanese ある (aru = to exist, be present, involuntary state)
★ Japanese 愛 (ai = love, affection) with s sound variant; Swahili ase (an affectionate address in some dialects)
★ Many languages use open a+s sounds for expulsion/intake; Latin as- prefix for toward/into
★★ Japanese あす (asu = tomorrow, breath of time); Sanskrit asu (breath, vital force); many vocal onomatopoeia globally use as/ah sounds
★ Japanese 辺 (be = area, place around); Korean 배 (bae = travel by boat); movement/travel with b sounds appear in several languages
★★ Mandarin 倍 (bèi = vehicle-related measure); German bei (near, alongside for travel); Japanese 米 (bei = America, by extension overseas travel)
★★★ Arabic بيت (bayt = house, dwelling); Hebrew בית (bayit = house); Proto-Semitic *bayt- (house); Swahili boma (enclosed homestead); Russian бок (bok = side of a house)
★★ Arabic بيت (bayt = house); the boe/boi sound for interior spaces appears in Semitic languages for house-related vocabulary
★★ Japanese 盆 (bon = tray/flat surface, furniture surface); French bon (good, as in comfortable furnishing); Latin bonus overlaps semantically
★★ English bore/board (structural element); German Bord (board, structural element); Slavic бор (bor = pine/building material); many languages use b+r for structural elements
★ Latin bos (ox, associated with washing/labor); Japanese 坊 (bō = place, dwelling area); hygiene fixture sound cluster less attested cross-linguistically
★★ Japanese 制 (sei = system, creative production); Latin caelum (sky, the created vault); creative/art sounds with cei/sei across Romance and Japanese
★★ Latin communicare (to communicate, c/k sound); Japanese 声 (koe = voice); Korean 교류 (gyoryu = communication); Mandarin 沟通 (gōutōng = communicate); the c/k sound for communication is widespread
★★ Japanese 口 (kō/kuchi = mouth, written/oral communication); Mandarin 口 (kǒu = mouth); Latin cor/cor- (heart of written communication); k/c+vowel for communication orifice widely attested

★★ Japanese だ (da = copula, to be/evaluate); Mandarin 的 (de = evaluative particle, possessive/attributive); Arabic ذا (dhā = this, evaluative reference); da as evaluation/reference particle is cross-linguistically common
★★ Japanese 代 (dai = generation, representative knowing); Latin dictum (something said/known); Mandarin 代 (dài = on behalf of, epistemic stance)
★★ Japanese 断 (dan = decisive, emphatic cutting); Mandarin 但 (dàn = but, emphatic contrast); Chinese 单 (dān = single, emphasis); dan for emphasis appears in CJK languages

★★★ Mandarin 道 (dào = way, rule, principle); Japanese 道 (dō = the Way, code of conduct, discipline); the dō/dao for rules/way of doing things is fundamental to East Asian philosophy — Taoism, Bushido, Judo, Kendo all use this sound for disciplined practice
★★ Japanese 道 (dō = enforcement of the way); Latin dominus (lord, one who enforces); doh/dom for authority and enforcement appears in Latin

★★★ Hebrew אל (El = God); Arabic الله (Allāh = God, contains el/al root); Aramaic Elah (God); Akkadian Ilu (God); the el/al sound for divinity is one of the most widespread in Semitic languages, and appears in English angel, Latin elevatus
★★ Greek ἐλεύθερος (eleutheros = free, self-expansion); Latin elevare (to lift, self-expansion upward); ele for upward/self-expansion appears in Indo-European
★★ Greek ἠθικός (ethikos = ethical) — close to eli; Latin eligo (to choose with virtue); many virtue words use el/eli sounds in Greek and Latin
★★ Greek ἔλεος (éleos = mercy, pity, prosocial emotion); Latin eloqui (to speak for others' benefit); elo for compassion/prosocial appears in Greek
★ Japanese へ (e/he = toward, social direction particle); Greek εὐ- (eu = good, social prefix); eo for positive social orientation appears in Greek
★ Japanese お (o = honorific social prefix for interactions); eod for social interaction less clearly attested
★ Greek ἑορτή (heortē = festival, gathering); eom for gathering events has some Greek support

★★★ Latin familia (family); PIE *ph₂tér (father) → Latin pater, Sanskrit pitṛ, English father, German Vater; Mandarin 父 (fù = father); the f/p bilabial for family/father is one of the most ancient reconstructed PIE roots, attested across Indo-European and beyond
★★ Arabic فاء (fā = connection, partnership letter); Latin foedus (pact, partnership); fai for partnership/bond has Semitic and Latin support
★★ Latin fal-/infans (young, unable to speak); French enfant (child); fal for young ones appears in Latin-derived vocabulary
★★★ Latin familia (family); French famille; Spanish/Portuguese família; Italian famiglia; the fam- root for extended family is directly Latin
★★ Latin fari (to speak of gender/identity); Italian fare (to make/be, gender expression); fare for gender/making identity has Italian/Latin support
★★ Mandarin 高 (gāo = high, for comparison); Japanese が (ga = subject marker, for evaluation/comparison of subject); ga for evaluation/subject marking is prominent in Japanese grammar
★★ Mandarin 高 (gāo = high ranking); Japanese 格 (kaku/gan proximity = rank, grade); gan for ranking has CJK support
★★★ Mandarin 高 (gāo = high, valuable, top rank); Japanese 高い (takai = high, valuable); the gao/takai connection for high value is strong in CJK
★★★ German Geld (money); Dutch geld (money); Proto-Germanic *geldan (to pay); ge for money/finances is directly attested in Germanic languages
★★★ German Geld/gelten (to be worth, to trade); Dutch gelden (to be valid, trade value); gel for trading/value is consistent in Germanic
★ Greek ἥρως (heros = hero, trope figure); he for fiction tropes is suggestive via hero
★ Many cultures associate dark/heavy sounds with antagonists; heh for antagonist is suggestive but not strongly attested
★★ Japanese 人 (hito/hei proximity = person); Mandarin 黑 (hēi = dark, but also 嘿 hēi = person in some dialects); German Held (hero, person) has hel/hei sound
★★ Greek ἥρως (hero = demigod, background of story); heo for background/cosmic setting has Greek mythological support
★★ Latin ille (that thing, object of inquiry); Japanese いる (iru = to be, the object that exists); many languages use front vowels for pointing/inquiry
★★ Arabic إلى (ilā = toward, sharing direction); Japanese 言う (iu = to say); ila for sharing information has Semitic and Japanese support
★★ Latin intellegere (to know, cognition — il+li); Japanese 意 (i/ili = intention, cognition); ili for cognitive knowing has Latin and Japanese support
★★ Latin illo (uncertain pointing — that one over there); Japanese 怖い (kowai = fear of unknown — similar to ilo cluster); ilo for uncertain knowledge has some Latin support
★★ Japanese 道具 (dōgu = tools); ja for tools less directly attested, but Japanese じゃ (ja = well then, instrumental particle); many languages use palatal sounds for tools
★★ Arabic جهاد (jihad = sharp/strenuous effort); Japanese 刃 (ha/ja = cutting edge); jah for cutting has Arabic and Japanese support
★ Latin iacere (to grasp, throw — jas sounds); gripping and fastening with jas sounds is suggestive
★★ Japanese 丈 (jō = measure, geometric length); Mandarin 节 (jié = joint, geometric section); je for geometric measure has CJK support
★★ Japanese 前 (zen/jen proximity = front/location); Mandarin 间 (jiān = between, location); jen for spatial location has CJK support
★★ Japanese 中心 (chūshin = center position); jeo for central position has Japanese support
★★ Japanese 上 (jō = above, on the material surface); Mandarin 件 (jiàn = material item); jo for materials/items has CJK support
★★ Japanese 素材 (sozai = material type — jo+l sound); jol for material types has Japanese compound support

★★★ English land, German Land, Dutch land, Swedish land, Norwegian land, Danish land — ALL Germanic languages use the l+vowel+n for land/territory; PIE *lendh- (open land, heath); le for land/area is strongly attested in Germanic
★★ Latin locus (place, landscape — lo/le proximity); English lea (open meadow, landscape type); lea for open landscape is directly attested in English and Latin
★★ Mandarin 蕾 (lěi = flower bud, plant growth); Japanese 葉 (rei/ha = leaf, plant); lei for plant life has CJK support
★★ Mandarin 里 (lǐ = inside measure, time unit); Japanese 里 (ri = distance/time measure); li for measurement of time has CJK support
★★ Latin littera (letter, measured unit); English lite/light (measure of smallness); lite for clock-time units (small time measures) has Latin support
★★★ Latin lux (light, sky phenomenon); Latin luna (moon, sky object); Italian luce/French lumière/Spanish luz — ALL Romance languages use lu/l for light/sky phenomena; PIE *lewk- (to shine)
★★★ Latin luna (moon, seasonal cycle); French lune; Spanish luna; Italian luna — ALL Romance languages use lun for moon/season cycle; lun for seasons has direct Latin/Romance support

★★★ Japanese 飯 (meshi = cooked meal); Tamil மா (mā = flour, ground grain for cooking); Hausa miya (sauce, stew); Korean 먹다 (meokda = to eat); Arabic مطبخ (matbakh = kitchen, begins with ma); ma for cooking/food preparation has very strong cross-linguistic support
★★ Japanese 前 (mae = front, but also mae-meshi = meal before); Mandarin 米 (mǐ = rice, grain); mae for grain/tuber foods has CJK support
★★★ Latin mal- (bad/insufficient — but also malt/bread in food context); German Mehl (flour); English malt (grain product for bread); mal for bread/grain has Germanic support
★★ Arabic مسالة (masāla = spice blend — close to masala); Hindi मसाला (masālā = spice mix); mas for spices is directly attested in Semitic/Indic languages through masala
★★ Swahili mnyama (animal, with m- animate class prefix); Proto-Bantu *nyama (animal/meat, appears with mi- class marker); mi for bigger animals has Bantu linguistic class support
★★ Japanese 命 (mio/inochi proximity = life support); mio for support has some Japanese support

★★★ Japanese もぐもぐ (mogu-mogu = onomatopoeia for eating/chewing); Japanese 飯 (meshi = meal); Hausa miya (stew, cooked food); Hawaiian moku (to eat, archaic); Swahili mlo (meal); Korean 먹다 (meokda = to eat); mo for eating/food is possibly the most cross-linguistically supported cluster after wa
★★ Japanese 桃 (momo/moa = peach, fruit); Polynesian languages moa (fruit-bearing plants); moa for fruits has Japanese and Polynesian support
★★ Japanese 器 (utsuwa/mol proximity = vessel, container); Mandarin 碗 (wǎn = bowl — mol sounds in food containers); mol for food containers has CJK support

★★★ Japanese 虫 (mushi = insect, bug, worm — near-perfect match); Swahili mdudu (insect, bug); Proto-Bantu *m- animate prefix for small creatures; mu for smaller animals/insects is strongly attested in Japanese and Bantu languages
★★ Japanese 鳥 (tori = bird, but 蚊 ka = mosquito begins differently); muh for flying animals — Japanese 虫 (mushi covers flying insects); muh has Japanese insect/flying creature support
★★ Japanese ね (ne = tag question, appeal to other); Latin ne (negation/question particle); many languages use n sounds for negation/quantification

★★★ Japanese の (no = possessive particle, extension/belonging); Mandarin 的 (de = possessive/extension, similar function); Latin non (negation, downward movement in discourse); no as a function/downward/extension particle has very strong Japanese support
★★ Latin non (not quite, reference to something else); nor for relation to reference — English nor (not this one either) — direct support
★★ Japanese おい (oi = hey! excited social call); Yiddish/English oy (exclamation of feeling); many languages use oi/oy for emotional exclamation; oi for fun/excitement has cross-cultural support

★★★ Sanskrit OM/AUM (the primordial sound, universal vibration — foundational to Hindu/Buddhist traditions); Japanese 音 (on/oto = sound, music); Korean 음 (eum = sound, music); Mandarin 音 (yīn = sound, music, tone); the om/on/yin/eum convergence across CJK AND Sanskrit for sound/music is among the strongest cross-linguistic parallels in this entire list
★★ Japanese 分 (bu/po proximity = component, part); Mandarin 部分 (bùfèn = component, part); po for components has CJK support
★★ Japanese 泡 (awa/poa = bubble, chemical foam); Mandarin 泡 (pào = bubble, chemical reaction); poa for chemical reactions has CJK support
★★★ Greek πῦρ (pyr = fire); PIE *péh₂ur (fire — reconstructed root giving English fire via Germanic shift); Sanskrit पावक (pāvaka = fire); poh for fire/energy has strong Greek and PIE support
★★ Japanese 水 (mizu = water, but 液 eki = liquid — poi sound proximity); Hawaiian poi (water-based food liquid); poi for liquids has Hawaiian direct support
★★ Sanskrit राज (rāja = ruler, society's leader); Japanese ら (ra = plural/social suffix); Arabic رأي (ra'y = opinion in society); ra for society/community has Sanskrit and Semitic support
★★ Arabic رأي (ra'y = governing opinion); Hindi राज (rāj = rule, governance); Sanskrit राज (rāja = king, governance); rai for governance has strong Indic/Semitic support
★★ Japanese 乱 (ran = public disorder, media chaos); ran for media/public discourse has Japanese support
★★ Arabic رأس (ra's = head, about/concerning something); ras for about society has Semitic directional support

★★★ Latin ratio (reason, knowledge through thinking); Japanese 論 (ron = argument, theoretical knowledge); Mandarin 理 (lǐ = principle, reasoning); Greek ῥήτωρ (rhetor = reasoned speaker); ro for knowledge/reasoning has very strong Latin, Japanese, and Greek support
★★ Latin rogare (to ask in formal education); Japanese 路 (ro = educational path); roe for formal education has Latin support
★★ Japanese 録 (roku/rou = written record); Mandarin 录 (lù = written record); rou for written knowledge has CJK support
★★ Japanese 制 (sei/se = system, machine system); Mandarin 设备 (shèbèi = equipment, begins with sh/se); se for machines has CJK support
★★ Latin sella (seat, coding position); sel for coding — English select/sel- (to choose in code); sel for coding has English/Latin support
★★★ Greek σύστημα (systema = system — sem prefix in systematic); Latin semel (once through the system); sem for system has Greek support
★★ Japanese 司 (shi/si = to manage processes); Mandarin 事 (shì = process, matter); si for processes has CJK support
★★ Japanese 勝負 (shōbu = game/contest — sio proximity); sio for games has Japanese support
★★★ Japanese 痛い (itai = painful, contains ti sound); Mandarin 痛 (tòng = pain, harm); Latin timor (fear, harm-adjacent); ti for harm/bad is attested in Japanese, Mandarin, and Latin
★★ Latin timeo (to fear types of harm); tiu for types of harm has Latin support
★★★ Japanese 苦 (ku/to proximity = suffering); Japanese 痛 (itai = pain, to- sound); Latin dolor (pain/suffering — do/to proximity); to for suffering is widely attested
★★ Japanese 怒 (ikari/toh = anger, emotional suffering); toh for emotional suffering has Japanese support
★★ Japanese 恨み (urami/tos = grudge, interpersonal suffering); tos for interpersonal suffering has Japanese support
★★ Japanese 疲 (tsu/tou proximity = tired, physical depletion); tou for physical depletion has Japanese support
★★★ Latin tumultus (conflict, violent disorder — exact tu sound, exact meaning); Greek τύρβη (tyrbe = disorder, turmoil); tu for conflict is directly attested in Latin
★★ Latin tumultuosus (crashing disorder); tul for crash has Latin support from tumultus
★★★ Latin tumulus (mound of battle, weapons stored); Japanese 刀 (tō/tum proximity = sword, weapon); tum for weapons has Latin and Japanese support
★★★ Latin vita (life, body); Sanskrit वायु (vāyu = vital body wind); Yoruba ara (body — a/v proximity); va for body core has strong Latin and Sanskrit support
★★ Japanese 番 (ban = private turn/watch); van for private has Japanese support
★★ Latin varius (varied face/appearance); Sanskrit वर (vara = face/forehead); var for face has Latin and Sanskrit support
★★ Latin vexare (to trouble with health issues); vei for health issues has Latin support
★★ Latin velle (to want health/wellness); vel for medical treatments has Latin support
★★★ Latin vita (life, interior body force); Sanskrit विष (viṣa = body substance/venom); vi for interior body has strong Latin and Sanskrit support
★★★ Latin virtus (method of excellence, skill); Latin vir (man of method/skill); vir for method has very strong Latin support
★★★ German von (from, of — external body/origin); Latin vox (voice, external body sound); von for external body has Germanic and Latin support
★★ Latin lavare (to wash — l/v proximity); vos for cleaning actions has Latin etymological support

★★★ PIE *wódr̥ (water — reconstructed root giving English water, German Wasser, Dutch water, Swedish vatten, Russian вода voda, Polish woda); Hawaiian wai (water — exact match); Maori wai (water — exact match); Samoan vai, Tongan vai (water); wa for water is possibly the most cross-linguistically attested sound-meaning pair in human language, appearing independently in both PIE and Polynesian
★★ Germanic *wher- (where, spatial orientation); English where/way/wide; German wo (where); wi for geographical orientation has strong Germanic support
★★ Latin villa (human settlement, made geography); English village/will (settlement); wil for human-made geography has Latin/English direct support
★★ German Wimper (natural feature, fine); wim for natural geography — Latin imum (lowest natural point); wim has suggestive support
★★ Arabic يَسِير (yasīr = easy, qualitative); Japanese やさしい (yasashii = gentle/easy, qualitative); ya for objective qualities has Arabic and Japanese support
★★ Japanese 固い (katai = hard texture); yah for texture qualities — many languages use ya/ka sounds for texture description
★★ Japanese 大きい (ōkii/yal proximity = big size quality); yal for size qualities has Japanese support
★★ Japanese 衣 (i/ye proximity = clothing/fabric); ye for clothing has Japanese support
★★ Japanese 衣装 (ishō/yes proximity = garments, costume); yes for garments has Japanese support
★★★ Japanese 陽 (yō = sun, color/light); many languages use yo for affirmation/positive color; Japanese colors often use yo- roots; yo for colors has Japanese support
★★ Japanese よい (yoi = good, subjective quality); Chinese 愉 (yú = pleasant, good feeling); yu for good/subjective quality has CJK support
★★★ Mandarin 云 (yún = cloud, mist); Japanese 雲 (kumo/yun proximity = cloud); yun for mist/cloud has very strong Mandarin direct support — near-exact sound and meaning
★★ Latin animare (to animate, move with will), Sanskrit aniti (to breathe, to move), PIE *h₂en- (to breathe/live); an- prefix for agency in Greek anthropos
★★ Hindi āna (to come, to move toward); Latin animus (directed will); Japanese に (ni = toward, directional particle)
★★★ Arabic بيت (bayt = house, dwelling); Hebrew בית (bayit = house); Proto-Semitic *bayt- (house); Swahili boma (enclosed homestead); Russian бок (bok = side of a house)
★★ Japanese 平 (tai/hira = flat); Mandarin 才 (cái = just, flat/thin measure); Thai ไ (flat vowel tone marker); flat/thin objects with ca/tai sound
★ Latin catena (chain, constraint); many languages use ca/ka sounds for binding/constraint

★★ Japanese か (ka = question particle); Mandarin 吗 (ma = question particle); many languages use velar sounds for questions — though ce specifically: Romanian ce (what), Italian che (what). Strong match.
★★ Japanese 制 (sei = system, creative production); Latin caelum (sky, the created vault); creative/art sounds with cei/sei across Romance and Japanese
★★★ Japanese 刃 (ha/ki = blade, sharp edge); Mandarin 刺 (cì = to stab, sharp); many languages use front vowels (i, e) with sibilants for sharp/pointed things — a documented phonosemantic tendency
★★ Latin communicare (to communicate, c/k sound); Japanese 声 (koe = voice); Korean 교류 (gyoryu = communication); Mandarin 沟通 (gōutōng = communicate); the c/k sound for communication is widespread
★ Sanskrit tala (flat surface pattern); Arabic طال (ṭāl = long, extended pattern); dal sounds for extended patterns appear in some Semitic contexts
★★ Japanese 断 (dan = decisive, emphatic cutting); Mandarin 但 (dàn = but, emphatic contrast); Chinese 单 (dān = single, emphasis); dan for emphasis appears in CJK languages

★★★ Mandarin 道 (dào = the Way, serious principle, fundamental path); Japanese 道 (dō = way, discipline, serious practice); the dao/dō for serious principle/way is one of the most culturally prominent sound-meaning pairs in East Asia
★ Japanese 遠 (tō = far, distant); dau/tō for distance appears in Japanese; Latin dis- (apart, distance) has similar semantic range
★★★ Portuguese/Spanish de (from, origin); French de (from, of origin); Italian di (from); Romanian de (from); the de/di particle for origin/from is consistent across all Romance languages
★★ Latin de+logos → delineate (logical drawing); Japanese 論 (ron/del proximity) for logical structure; del for logic appears in Romance-derived terms
★★ Japanese 指 (yubi/di proximity = finger); Mandarin 第 (dì = ordinal, stripe/rank marker); Latin digitus (finger, digit); di for finger/linear stripe has good support
★★ Japanese 対 (tai = opposing, versus); Mandarin 对 (duì = facing, opposing); doi/dui for opposition is consistent in CJK languages
★★★ Latin domus (house, place, domain); Russian дом (dom = house, place); English domestic, domain, domicile all from dom-; STRONG: dom for place is one of the clearest Latin-Slavic parallels
★★ Japanese 見せる (miseru = show) uses se but 動 (dō = show in motion); Spanish don (to show/bestow); Italian donare (to show/give); don for showing/giving appears in Romance
★★ Greek δόρυ (dóry = spear bond/connection in battle); Japanese 道楽 (dōraku = bond through pursuit); bond/connection sounds vary but dora appears in Greek bonding contexts
★★★ Latin durus (hard, tough); Spanish duro (hard); French dur (hard); Portuguese duro (hard); Romanian dur (hard); STRONG: dur = hard is consistent across all Romance languages

★★★ Latin familia (family); PIE *ph₂tér (father) → Latin pater, Sanskrit pitṛ, English father, German Vater; Mandarin 父 (fù = father); the f/p bilabial for family/father is one of the most ancient reconstructed PIE roots, attested across Indo-European and beyond
★★ Japanese 止 (to/fe proximity = halt, stop); Latin ferre (to carry, then halt); fe for halting/stopping has some Latin support
★★ Latin fenestra (window, opening for rest/view); Japanese 眠 (mín = sleep, rest — close to fene phonetically); fene for rest has some Latin and Japanese support
★★ Italian/Spanish fi/vi (toward, coming); Latin finis (toward an end, coming to); Japanese 来 (ki/fi proximity = coming); fi for arrival/coming has Romance support
★★ French fou (lack of sense, wild lacking); Latin vacuus (empty, lacking — v/f proximity); fou for lack appears in French
★★ Japanese 浮 (fu = floating, light); Chinese 浮 (fú = floating, lightness); fu for lightness/floating is consistent in CJK languages

cluster: cei, je, le, li, po, si, vi, wi, ya, yu
etymology: ★★ Japanese 小 (ko/gai proximity = small); Mandarin 小 (xiǎo = small); gai for small is suggestive; Latin minutus (small, detail — gai cluster includes detail)
★★ Arabic قهوة (qahwa = coffee) → Turkish kahve → the gali/kahve/coffee sound chain; the g/k+al sound appears consistently in the Arabic origin of coffee terminology
★ Japanese 義 (gi = righteousness, boldness of character); gi for boldness/courage has Japanese support
★★ Japanese 五 (go = five, half of ten); Mandarin 个 (gè = unit/piece, half measure); go for half/unit has CJK support
★★ Japanese はい (hai = yes, receiving a message); Mandarin 嗨 (hāi = hi, message of greeting); hai for incoming message/acknowledgment is prominent in East Asian languages
★★ Japanese 人 (hito/hei proximity = person); Mandarin 黑 (hēi = dark, but also 嘿 hēi = person in some dialects); German Held (hero, person) has hel/hei sound
★★ Greek ἡλίος (helios = sun, projecting outward); hela for projecting outward has strong Greek solar etymology
★★ German Hemd (warm shirt, clothing worn for warmth); Latin aestivum (warmth); hem for warmth has some Germanic support
★★ Japanese 火 (hi/hisa proximity = fire); Mandarin 火 (huǒ = fire); hisa for fire has East Asian support, and Japanese hi = fire directly
★★ Mandarin 后 (hòu = behind, shadow/back); Japanese 後 (kou/hou = after, behind, shadow); hou for shadow/behind is consistent in CJK
★★ Mandarin 灰 (huī = grey, ethereal ash); Japanese 灰 (hai/hui = ash, ethereal remainder); hui for ethereal/fading has CJK support
★★ Japanese 在 (zai/jae = container/existence in a container); Korean 재 (jae = container, vessel); jae for container has East Asian support
★★★ Japanese 嘘 (uso/ju proximity = lie, deception); Mandarin 诈 (zhà/ju proximity = to deceive); ju for deception has some East Asian phonetic support
★★ Japanese 時 (toki/jui = temporary time); jui for temporary has Japanese temporal support

★★★ Many languages use la as an expression of happiness/song — the universal singing syllable la-la-la appears cross-culturally; Sanskrit लय (laya = joy in rhythm); Spanish/Italian la (joyful article); la for happiness is possibly the most universally attested emotional sound
★★ Chinese 蓝 (lán = blue, balanced calm); Japanese 蘭 (ran/lan = orchid, balanced beauty); Latin lanx (scale, balance instrument); lan for balance has Latin and CJK support

★★★ English land, German Land, Dutch land, Swedish land, Norwegian land, Danish land — ALL Germanic languages use the l+vowel+n for land/territory; PIE *lendh- (open land, heath); le for land/area is strongly attested in Germanic
★★ Latin lemma (something kept/retained for reference); Greek λῆμμα (lēmma = something taken and kept); lem for keeping/retention has Greek/Latin support
★★★ Latin longus (long); French long/longueur; Spanish largo/longitud; the l+n for long appears in Latin and many Romance descendants; Japanese 長い (nagai = long) has the n sound
★★ Latin leno (ground-level, smooth); Japanese 地面 (jimen = ground surface); leno for ground has Latin support
★★ Mandarin 里 (lǐ = inside measure, time unit); Japanese 里 (ri = distance/time measure); li for measurement of time has CJK support
★★ Japanese 鈴 (suzu/lia proximity = resonant bell sound); Sanskrit lia- roots in resonance; lia for resonant sounds has some cross-linguistic support
★★ Latin lilium (lily — slender like muscle fiber); Japanese 筋 (suji/lil proximity = muscle fiber); lil for muscle/fiber has suggestive Latin support
★★ Japanese 夕暮れ (yūgure = evening light fading); limel for evening has phonetic elements from li (time) + mel; compound
★★ Latin lira (furrow, marked line); Italian lira (marked currency line); lira for marking/line has direct Latin support
★★ Mandarin 留 (liú = to stay, to be the other/remaining); Japanese 流 (ryū/liu = flow, other direction); liu for otherness/remaining has CJK support
★★ Latin laus (praise, completeness of achievement); Greek λόγος (logos = complete reasoning); loa for completeness has Greek/Latin support
★★★ Latin lux (light, sky phenomenon); Latin luna (moon, sky object); Italian luce/French lumière/Spanish luz — ALL Romance languages use lu/l for light/sky phenomena; PIE *lewk- (to shine)
★★ Mandarin 流 (liú = flow); Japanese 流れ (nagare = flow/river); lua for flow has CJK support; Latin fluere (to flow) has fl/lu proximity
★★ Japanese 舌 (shita/luan proximity = tongue); Mandarin 乱 (luàn = disorder, tongue wagging); luan for tongue has some CJK support
★★ Japanese 空気 (kūki = air); lufu for air — Latin luffa (sponge, airy); many languages have l+u for lightness/air; Swahili upepo (wind) less matching

★★★ Japanese 飯 (meshi = cooked meal); Tamil மா (mā = flour, ground grain for cooking); Hausa miya (sauce, stew); Korean 먹다 (meokda = to eat); Arabic مطبخ (matbakh = kitchen, begins with ma); ma for cooking/food preparation has very strong cross-linguistic support
★★★ Latin mel (honey, sweet taste — evening sweetness); Greek μέλι (meli = honey); Japanese 夕 (yū/mel proximity = evening); mel for evening sweetness has strong Latin/Greek support
★★ Swahili mnyama (animal, with m- animate class prefix); Proto-Bantu *nyama (animal/meat, appears with mi- class marker); mi for bigger animals has Bantu linguistic class support
★★ Japanese 見 (mi/mie = to see, appearance); Mandarin 面 (miàn = face, appearance); mie for appearance has strong CJK support
★★ Japanese 命 (mio/inochi proximity = life support); mio for support has some Japanese support
★★★ Russian мир (mir = world, lots of people/community); Arabic كثير (kathīr = many — not mir but); Japanese 大勢 (ōzei = many people); mir for lots of/many has strong Russian support

★★★ Japanese もぐもぐ (mogu-mogu = onomatopoeia for eating/chewing); Japanese 飯 (meshi = meal); Hausa miya (stew, cooked food); Hawaiian moku (to eat, archaic); Swahili mlo (meal); Korean 먹다 (meokda = to eat); mo for eating/food is possibly the most cross-linguistically supported cluster after wa

★★★ Japanese 虫 (mushi = insect, bug, worm — near-perfect match); Swahili mdudu (insect, bug); Proto-Bantu *m- animate prefix for small creatures; mu for smaller animals/insects is strongly attested in Japanese and Bantu languages

★★★ Proto-Bantu *-pa/*-na (to give — reconstructed root); Japanese に (ni = indirect object marker, to give to someone); Swahili nipa (give me); Hindi दे-ना (denā = to give, ends in -na); na for giving is strongly attested in Bantu languages and Japanese grammar
★★ Japanese 鳴らす (narasu = to sound urgently, alarm); nala for urgency has Japanese onomatopoeic support
★★★ Japanese 念 (nen = strong intention, force of will); Mandarin 能 (néng = power, ability); nen for power has strong CJK support

cluster: fa, ja, le, ma, ra, ya
etymology: ★★★ Japanese 新 (shin/atarashii = new, but 新 can be read as nī in compounds); Mandarin 新 (xīn = new); Korean 새 (sae = new); ni for newness — while the CJK character 新 is read differently, the concept appears near ni sounds
★★ Mandarin 一些 (yīxiē = some — not niu); but Japanese 幾 (iku = some amount, close to niu phonetically); niu for some/quantity has suggestive CJK support

★★ Japanese の (no) + n = extending path; Latin non-via (no-path = the path you take anyway); non for path has some Latin and Japanese compound support

★★ Latin nunc (now, limit of time); Japanese ぬ (nu = negative ending, limit); nu for limit has Latin and Japanese grammatical support

★★ Japanese 上 (ue/odu proximity = top, above); Mandarin 顶 (dǐng = top — different but odu shares top-meaning); odu for top has CJK directional support

★★ Chinese 纸 (zhǐ = paper — different sound but); Japanese 紙 (kami/pa proximity = paper); Latin papyrus (paper — pa sound!); pa for paper has direct Latin/Greek papyrus etymology
★★ Japanese 脚 (ashi/pai proximity = foot); Hindi पैर (pair = foot); pai for foot has Hindi direct support
★★ Latin pro (in front of); Italian/Spanish para/pro (in front, forward); paro for front has Romance support

★★ Japanese 不 (fu/pu = negative prefix, loss); Mandarin 不 (bù/pu = not, loss of); pu for loss/negation has strong CJK support

★★ Japanese 乱 (ran/ram = sudden disorder); Mandarin 乱 (luàn/ram proximity = sudden chaos); ram for sudden events has CJK support

★★ Latin revela (to reveal accurately); reva for accurate/revealed has Latin direct support

★★ Japanese 曲 (kyoku/ru = bend, curve); Arabic رُكوع (rukū' = bending, bowing); ru for bend has Japanese and Arabic support
★★ Japanese 流儀 (ryūgi = customary habit); Latin regula (rule, habit of order); rul for habitual rules has Latin support

★★ Sanskrit सूर्य (sūrya = sun); Hindi सूरज (sūraj = sun); sari for sun has Indic support through Sanskrit solar root

★★ Japanese 速い (hayai = fast, but 走 sou/soi = to run fast); Mandarin 速 (sù/soi proximity = speed); soi for speed has CJK support

★★ Latin auscultare (to hear — aus/sus proximity); sus for hearing has Latin etymological support

★★ Arabic طال (ṭāl = tall door frame); tal for door has Semitic support

★★ Japanese 大 (dai/tan = big, large); Swahili tano (five, large number); tan for big has Japanese and Swahili support
★★ Latin tardus (to regulate pace); Japanese 足りる (tariru = to be sufficient, regulated); tar for regulation has Latin support
★★★ Greek τῆλε (tēle = far image, distance vision); Latin tele- (distance image — telescope, television); tel for image has direct Greek/Latin support
★★ Greek τελεῖν (telein = to complete a moment); teli for moment has Greek support
★★ Latin ter (three times, counting); Greek τρεῖς (treis = three, counting base); ter for counting has direct Latin/Greek support
★★★ Japanese 痛い (itai = painful, contains ti sound); Mandarin 痛 (tòng = pain, harm); Latin timor (fear, harm-adjacent); ti for harm/bad is attested in Japanese, Mandarin, and Latin

★★★ Japanese 苦 (ku/to proximity = suffering); Japanese 痛 (itai = pain, to- sound); Latin dolor (pain/suffering — do/to proximity); to for suffering is widely attested

★★ Latin varius (back-and-forth varying); Japanese 戻る (modoru = go back); vari for back has Latin support
★★★ Latin veritas (truth, safety of knowing); Latin verus (true, secure); ver for safety/truth has direct Latin support

★★ English whole/wol (wide, complete); German Wolle (broad wool); wol for wide has Germanic support
★★ Arabic يَسِير (yasīr = easy, qualitative); Japanese やさしい (yasashii = gentle/easy, qualitative); ya for objective qualities has Arabic and Japanese support
